attributes.stem: A 25-year-old man from the local prison is brought to the Emergency Department complaining of acute abdominal pain. He states that the previous day he had intentionally swallowed some pieces of glass. This is not the first time this event has been recorded. He has no other significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His abdomen is soft and non-tender. An abdominal X-ray is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
In adults, foreign bodies are swallowed either deliberately or accidentally. In that latter group are those that might have been intoxicated, have limited capacity or an unawareness of a fish or chicken bone in their food. Those that willfully swallow foreign objects may be individuals wanting secondary gain with mental health conditions, or incarcerated individuals. The latter group will often be repeat offenders. Often the so-called 'sharp' object is wrapped in tape.

In this case, the X-ray shows the foreign bodies appear to be in the descending colon, well beyond the stomach and duodenum. This location means they are not accessible via endoscopy and have already navigated the narrowest parts of the upper gastrointestinal tract. While X-ray cannot fully assess whether objects are sharp, it is somewhat reassuring that they appear relatively rounded on imaging rather than having obvious sharp edges.

In most cases no form of intervention is required and no hospital care is necessary. Those objects that are more likely to need active intervention are ones more than 6 cm in length and those that have not moved for a week or so. The rate of perforation or obstruction for sharp objects (razors, batteries) does not appear to be any worse than for any other foreign body.

In the case presented here, these foreign bodies appear to be in the descending colon and will almost certainly pass on their own accord and can be left well alone.

Watchful waiting is suitable as up to 97% of ingested foreign bodies will pass spontaneously. The swallowed objects appear to be well-rounded and they do not appear to have sharp edges. They have also extended beyond the stomach and duodenum, indicating that they are able to pass through the gastrointestinal tract without causing harm or obstruction.

Conservative management (watchful waiting) is indicated for ingested objects that are short, blunt and narrow, as these objects appear. The patient must be monitored for acute pain, signs of shock, repeat imaging and stool examination to ensure passage of the ingested objects. In most circumstances this monitoring can be done at home, with the patient given instructions on what symptoms would indicate a return for hospital review.

Ipecac is an emetic that was once used to induce vomiting in patients that had ingested poison, however it is no longer recommended due to lack of effectiveness as well as risk of toxicity.

Polyethylene glycol solution and magnesium citrate solution are laxative medications that are used as bowel preparation before gastrointestinal procedures. They are not indicated for treatment of foreign body ingestion.

Endoscopy is not recommended as data suggests that it fails almost 50% of the time as the ingested contents are not reachable by endoscopy, and almost all ingested materials will pass spontaneously. This also subjects the patient to anaesthetic risks that are likely not necessary. Endoscopy is indicated if the ingested object is proximal enough to be reached and is potentially dangerous eg. sharp, batteries, magnets to cause secondary complications.
learningPoints: Watchful waiting is appropriate for ingested foreign objects as 97% will pass spontaneously without intervention.
Endoscopy for foreign body retrieval is indicated only when objects are potentially dangerous (sharp, batteries, magnets) and/ or easily accessible.
Regular clinical and radiological monitoring is essential during conservative management of foreign body ingestion.
references: Weiland ST, Schurr MJ. Conservative management of ingested foreign bodies. J Gastrointest Surg. 2002 Jun;6(3):496-500. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12022984

Birk M, Bauerfeind P, Deprez PH, Häfner M, Hartmann D, Hassan C, Hucl T, Lesur G, Aabakken L, Meining A. Removal of foreign bodies in the upper gastrointestinal tract in adults: European Society of Gastrointestinal Endoscopy (ESGE) Clinical Guideline. Endoscopy. 2016 May;48(5):489-96. doi: 10.1055/s-0042-100456. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26862844

Ambe P, Weber SA, Schauer M, Knoefel WT. Swallowed foreign bodies in adults. Dtsch Arztebl Int. 2012 Dec;109(50):869-75. doi: 10.3238/arztebl.2012.0869. Epub 2012 Dec 14. PMID: 23293675; PMCID: PMC3536040. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23293675/
